title: An AI agent ran a full ransomware attack by itself - TCR 07/07/26
description:
Sysdig documented JadePuffer, the first ransomware attack an AI agent ran end to end, encrypting 1,300+ records and writing its own note, Illinois signed SB 315 requiring third-party frontier audits and 72-hour incident reporting, and Anthropic's Fable agent wrote the fastest GPU megakernel ever submitted at an 18.71x speedup.

https://sharedsapience.com/the-century-report-july-7-2026/

0:00 JadePuffer, the first AI-run ransomware attack
0:24 Microsoft cuts and the AI-era labor shift
3:12 Governance moves at three altitudes
6:32 Fable writes the fastest kernel ever submitted
9:07 The grid, the courts, and the heat push back
12:28 Closing and Synthesis

This episode covers the JadePuffer autonomous ransomware operation, Illinois's SB 315 frontier AI law, the UN Global Dialogue on AI Governance in Geneva, Anthropic's Fable megakernel record, agentic AI's 136.5x energy draw against a $19 billion Kentucky lease, and Microsoft's 4,800 layoffs inside a 120,000-job tech year.

Sysdig's researchers say JadePuffer swept a compromised server, moved laterally, exfiltrated data, encrypted more than 1,300 configuration records, and wrote its own ransom note, self-correcting a code error in 31 seconds - dropping the skill floor for the crime to near zero. That same autonomy runs the other direction: Anthropic's Fable agent produced the fastest megakernel submitted to KernelBench-Mega at 18.71x, beating every frontier peer at optimizing the compute models run on. Governance moved at three altitudes at once, as Illinois signed the strongest US frontier AI law, Australia's safety institute named deceptive model behavior as goal-seeking under constraint, and the first government-level UN dialogue opened in Geneva. Underneath runs the physical bill: agentic systems can draw up to 136.5 times the energy of a single-shot model, Anthropic committed $19 billion over 20 years for Kentucky power, Duke Energy moved to bill data centers directly, and a heatwave downed the UK's Dawn supercomputer. Microsoft cut roughly 4,800 roles, while research on 22,000 firms found heavy AI adopters grew headcount 10.2%.
